dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 12 responding for a suspected diabetic issue at a residence in Bellwood.
Hi, I'm Mark. I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
My mom's been acting sick all day. She felt extremely tired, was sweating a lot, and was too weak to eat.
Was she conscious and breathing when you called?
Yes, she was sitting in bed and breathing. She was alert, but confused about what time it was.
Can you keep your eyes open and follow my finger?
(Eyes follow the finger briefly, then drift closed slightly)
What is your name and where are we right now?
Mary... somewhere in my house.
Mary, can you squeeze my hand for me?
(Squeezes firmly but slowly)
Your airway is clear, Mary. I just want to confirm a few things, okay?
(Nods slowly)
Patient's breathing is clear and even in both lungs.
Blood pressure is 176 over 75 and her pulse is 62. She's very cold and pale. She seems to be sweating a lot.
Was there anything about her prescribed medications or insulin dose today?
She took her insulin, but she didn't eat. She wasn't feeling well all morning.
Do you know if she has any known drug allergies?
No, she doesn't have any allergies that we know of.
Mary, can you tell us your full name and your age?
Mary Smith. I'm 56.
Can you tell us about any past medical issues or conditions you have?
Let me check... She's on dialysis every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday. She was just diagnosed with non-insulin-dependent diabetes.
On a scale of 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst pain you've ever had, can you rate your pain?
I don't really feel any pain, but I feel extremely tired and weak.
Did the weakness start suddenly or gradually?
It came on slowly all day.
When did you first start feeling sick or weak?
She said she felt unwell all day. This is the first time she was too weak to stay up.
Ma'am, we're going to check your blood sugar. You'll feel a slight pinch in just a second.
Her initial blood sugar is 43. That's quite low.
We're going to place monitoring leads to check her heart now, okay, Mary?
Initial heartbeat reading shows sinus bradycardia.
We need to give you some glucose to help raise your blood sugar quickly. Can we do that?
(Nods slowly)
Here's 15 grams of oral glucose. You'll feel it now.
Let's recheck her blood sugar in a bit. It was a low 43. Keep her warm.
We're following the protocol for diabetic hypoglycemia.
Her nose and mouth are clear of any signs of injury or blockage.
Her head and neck are normal. Pupils are equal and reactive, and she's not showing signs of swallowing difficulty.
Her peripheral pulses are strong and regular. No deformities or angulation observed.
She is pale, cold, and very sweaty. Skin is diaphoretic.
Her pulse is 55 now, down from 62. Respiration is 28.
Blood pressure is 171 over 80.
Blood sugar is still 45.
Oxygen saturation is 100. No issues noted.
The ecg is still showing sinus bradycardia. No change.
We're now setting up for an IV to give Dextrose 10% in case the glucose doesn't help.
IV access is successfully placed in the right wrist.
Pulse is 57, respiration is 17, blood pressure is 157 over 85. No pain reported.
Blood sugar remains at 0, oxygen is 100. Bradycardia still present. Respiratory rate is 17.
We'll give her IV dextrose 10% now, okay?
Infusion started. Monitoring closely for response.
Blood sugar is still 0, pulse at 54, respiration 16, blood pressure 174 over 97. She reports no pain.
EKG still shows no significant changes. Bradycardia continues.
I'm going to do a 12-lead ECG now to rule out any cardiac issues.
12-lead ECG was obtained. It shows sinus bradycardia with occasional supraventricular premature complexes.
Pulse 56, respiration 14, BP 169 over 75. No pain reported.
Blood sugar remains at 0 despite previous glucose and Dextrose 10% administration.
Pulse is 58, respiration 20, BP 187 over 65. Bradycardia still noted on EKG.
Her blood sugar is now 245. That's an improvement.
Oxygen saturation is 98. She is not hypoxic at this time.
Next, we need to transport her to the hospital. VCU Health is accepting, and they specialize in manageable cases like this.
We're setting up the stretcher now. I'll call for ED to get ready. Once we load you on, we'll move carefully and safely.
All straps are secure, and the safety belts are fastened. She's ready for transport.
Alright, outside the ED. She's steady. IV still on, last glucose prior to arrival was at 245.
Got her. ECG, blood sugar, vitals all updated and charted on arrival. No immediate signs of acute cardiac issue.
No known allergies. Hypoglycemia was the primary concern. She's been given glucose and Dextrose 10% followed by a rise in elevation.
Latest blood sugar was 245. Looks like she's responding well. Keep her on IV fluids and continue monitoring.
Transport records were completed at the scene and during transport. Patient was documented prior to arrival.
Her arrival vitals matched with the ones we observed pre-transport. No signs of deterioration during the ride.
All transportation records were documented. Patient remained stable. No patient resistance or complaint from our crews.
Transport was emergent, and she was secured the entire trip. No patient resistance or complaint from our crews.
We've completed our care and given all treatment according to protocol.
Care is accepted at VCU Health. Next step is setting up dialysis monitoring and close ECG monitoring.
Completed protocol for diabetic hypoglycemia per standing orders during treatment and transport.
All documentation is submitted. Time metrics from arrival to dialysis and IV infusion documented.
Her SpO2 has remained consistently at 99% or higher during transport and preliminary hospital observation.
Recurs once again at 61, slow but regular. No irregularities observed via manual pulse confirmation.
Her glucose went from 43 at arrival to 245 prior to getting here. Good response from treatment.
Her respiratory rate was recorded at 16 at 20:44.
